IPL Data Analysis & Match Outcome Prediction

Yukesh

2026-04-09

1 Introduction

1.1 Project Overview

The Indian Premier League (IPL) is the world’s most popular Twenty20 cricket league, played annually in India since 2008. This project performs a comprehensive data analysis of IPL matches from 2008 to 2024 and builds machine learning models to predict match outcomes.

1.2 Objectives

The key objectives of this project are:

  • Explore and visualize IPL match data across 17 seasons
  • Identify key patterns in team performance, toss decisions, and venue statistics
  • Engineer meaningful features from raw match data
  • Build and compare three machine learning models to predict which team wins a match
  • Evaluate model performance and identify the most important predictive features

1.3 Dataset Description

Two datasets were used from Kaggle’s IPL Complete Dataset:

  • matches.csv — 1,095 rows, one per match, covering season, teams, toss, venue, and result
  • deliveries.csv — 260,920 rows, one per ball bowled, covering batsman, bowler, runs, and dismissals

2 Setup & Libraries

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(caret)
library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
library(randomForest)
library(corrplot)
library(pROC)

3 Data Loading & Exploration

matches    <- read_csv("../data/matches_clean.csv")
deliveries <- read_csv("../data/deliveries_clean.csv")

cat("Matches loaded   :", nrow(matches), "rows,", ncol(matches), "cols\n")
## Matches loaded   : 1090 rows, 18 cols
cat("Deliveries loaded:", nrow(deliveries), "rows,", ncol(deliveries), "cols\n")
## Deliveries loaded: 260920 rows, 17 cols

3.1 Dataset Structure

glimpse(matches)
## Rows: 1,090
## Columns: 18
## $ id              <dbl> 335982, 335983, 335984, 335985, 335986, 335987, 335988…
## $ season          <dbl> 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, …
## $ city            <chr> "Bangalore", "Chandigarh", "Delhi", "Mumbai", "Kolkata…
## $ date            <date> 2008-04-18, 2008-04-19, 2008-04-19, 2008-04-20, 2008-…
## $ match_type      <chr> "League", "League", "League", "League", "League", "Lea…
## $ player_of_match <chr> "BB McCullum", "MEK Hussey", "MF Maharoof", "MV Bouche…
## $ venue           <chr> "M Chinnaswamy Stadium", "Punjab Cricket Association S…
## $ team1           <chr> "Royal Challengers Bengaluru", "Punjab Kings", "Delhi …
## $ team2           <chr> "Kolkata Knight Riders", "Chennai Super Kings", "Rajas…
## $ toss_winner     <chr> "Royal Challengers Bengaluru", "Chennai Super Kings", …
## $ toss_decision   <chr> "field", "bat", "bat", "bat", "bat", "bat", "bat", "fi…
## $ winner          <chr> "Kolkata Knight Riders", "Chennai Super Kings", "Delhi…
## $ result          <chr> "runs", "runs", "wickets", "wickets", "wickets", "wick…
## $ result_margin   <dbl> 140, 33, 9, 5, 5, 6, 9, 6, 3, 66, 7, 9, 10, 4, 13, 7, …
## $ target_runs     <dbl> 223, 241, 130, 166, 111, 167, 143, 209, 215, 183, 136,…
## $ target_overs    <dbl> 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20…
## $ super_over      <chr> "N", "N", "N", "N", "N", "N", "N", "N", "N", "N", "N",…
## $ toss_winner_won <chr> "No", "Yes", "No", "No", "No", "No", "No", "No", "Yes"…

3.2 Missing Values

cat("Missing values in matches:\n")
## Missing values in matches:
colSums(is.na(matches))
##              id          season            city            date      match_type 
##               0               0               0               0               0 
## player_of_match           venue           team1           team2     toss_winner 
##               0               0               0               0               0 
##   toss_decision          winner          result   result_margin     target_runs 
##               0               0               0              14               0 
##    target_overs      super_over toss_winner_won 
##               0               0               0

4 Data Cleaning

The following cleaning steps were applied to the raw data:

  1. Season format standardized from mixed formats to 4-digit year
  2. Team names unified — 4 inconsistencies fixed (e.g. Kings XI Punjab → Punjab Kings)
  3. No-result matches removed (5 matches with no winner)
  4. Missing cities filled from venue names
  5. Irrelevant columns dropped (method, umpire1, umpire2)
  6. Over numbering corrected from 0–19 to 1–20
  7. Toss impact column added
cat("Final cleaned dataset dimensions:\n")
## Final cleaned dataset dimensions:
cat("Matches   :", nrow(matches), "rows,", ncol(matches), "cols\n")
## Matches   : 1090 rows, 18 cols
cat("Deliveries:", nrow(deliveries), "rows,", ncol(deliveries), "cols\n")
## Deliveries: 260920 rows, 17 cols

5 Exploratory Data Analysis

5.1 Most Successful Teams

team_wins <- matches %>%
  group_by(winner) %>%
  summarise(wins = n()) %>%
  arrange(desc(wins))

ggplot(team_wins, aes(x = reorder(winner, wins), y = wins, fill = wins)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#56B4E9", high = "#E69F00") +
  labs(
    title = "Total IPL Wins by Team (2008–2024)",
    x = "Team", y = "Number of Wins"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Total IPL wins by each team across all seasons

Key Finding: Mumbai Indians lead with 144 wins, followed closely by Chennai Super Kings (138) and Kolkata Knight Riders (131). These three teams form the “Big Three” of IPL.

5.2 Toss Decision Trend

toss_trend <- matches %>%
  group_by(season, toss_decision) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(toss_trend,
       aes(x = season, y = count,
           color = toss_decision, group = toss_decision)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("bat" = "#E69F00", "field" = "#0072B2")) +
  labs(
    title  = "Toss Decision Trend Across IPL Seasons",
    x = "Season", y = "Number of Matches", color = "Toss Decision"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

How toss decisions have changed across IPL seasons

Key Finding: Teams consistently prefer fielding first — 704 matches (64%) vs batting first (391 matches, 36%). This reflects T20 cricket’s chase-friendly nature.

5.3 Does Toss Winning Help?

toss_impact <- matches %>%
  group_by(toss_winner_won) %>%
  summarise(count = n()) %>%
  mutate(percentage = round(count / sum(count) * 100, 1))

ggplot(toss_impact,
       aes(x = toss_winner_won, y = count, fill = toss_winner_won)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(percentage, "%")),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Yes" = "#009E73", "No" = "#D55E00")) +
  labs(
    title = "Does Winning the Toss Lead to Winning the Match?",
    x = "Toss Winner Also Won?", y = "Number of Matches"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Relationship between winning the toss and winning the match

Key Finding: Toss winners win only 50.8% of the time — barely above a coin flip. This confirms that the toss has minimal impact on match outcomes in modern IPL cricket.

5.4 Batting First vs Chasing

win_type <- matches %>%
  filter(result %in% c("runs", "wickets")) %>%
  group_by(result) %>%
  summarise(count = n()) %>%
  mutate(
    label      = ifelse(result == "runs",
                        "Won Batting First", "Won Chasing"),
    percentage = round(count / sum(count) * 100, 1)
  )

ggplot(win_type, aes(x = "", y = count, fill = label)) +
  geom_col(width = 1) +
  coord_polar(theta = "y") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(label, "\n", percentage, "%")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Won Batting First" = "#E69F00",
               "Won Chasing"       = "#56B4E9")) +
  labs(title = "IPL Matches: Batting First vs Chasing Wins") +
  theme_void(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Distribution of wins by batting first versus chasing

5.5 Top 10 Run Scorers

top_batsmen <- deliveries %>%
  group_by(batter) %>%
  summarise(total_runs = sum(batsman_runs)) %>%
  arrange(desc(total_runs)) %>%
  slice(1:10)

ggplot(top_batsmen,
       aes(x = reorder(batter, total_runs),
           y = total_runs, fill = total_runs)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_text(aes(label = total_runs),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#56B4E9", high = "#D55E00") +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Run Scorers in IPL History",
    x = "Batsman", y = "Total Runs"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  ylim(0, max(top_batsmen$total_runs) * 1.1)

All-time top 10 run scorers in IPL history

Key Finding: Virat Kohli leads with 8,014 runs — 1,245 runs ahead of second-placed Shikhar Dhawan (6,769).

5.6 Top 10 Wicket Takers

top_bowlers <- deliveries %>%
  filter(is_wicket == 1) %>%
  filter(!dismissal_kind %in%
           c("run out", "retired hurt", "obstructing the field")) %>%
  group_by(bowler) %>%
  summarise(wickets = n()) %>%
  arrange(desc(wickets)) %>%
  slice(1:10)

ggplot(top_bowlers,
       aes(x = reorder(bowler, wickets),
           y = wickets, fill = wickets)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_text(aes(label = wickets),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#56B4E9", high = "#009E73") +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Wicket Takers in IPL History",
    x = "Bowler", y = "Total Wickets"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  ylim(0, max(top_bowlers$wickets) * 1.1)

All-time top 10 wicket takers in IPL history

Key Finding: Yuzvendra Chahal leads with 205 wickets, confirming that leg-spin bowling dominates T20 cricket.

5.7 Matches Per Season

season_matches <- matches %>%
  group_by(season) %>%
  summarise(total_matches = n())

ggplot(season_matches,
       aes(x = factor(season), y = total_matches, fill = total_matches)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = total_matches),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#56B4E9", high = "#E69F00") +
  labs(
    title = "Number of IPL Matches Played Per Season",
    x = "Season", y = "Total Matches"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

Growth of IPL season size over the years

5.8 Top 10 Venues

top_venues <- matches %>%
  group_by(venue) %>%
  summarise(matches_hosted = n()) %>%
  arrange(desc(matches_hosted)) %>%
  slice(1:10)

ggplot(top_venues,
       aes(x = reorder(venue, matches_hosted),
           y = matches_hosted, fill = matches_hosted)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_text(aes(label = matches_hosted),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#56B4E9", high = "#CC79A7") +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 IPL Venues by Matches Hosted",
    x = "Venue", y = "Matches Hosted"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  ylim(0, max(top_venues$matches_hosted) * 1.15)

Most frequently used IPL venues

Key Finding: Eden Gardens, Kolkata has hosted the most IPL matches (77), followed by Wankhede Stadium, Mumbai (74).

6 Feature Engineering

Eleven features were engineered from the raw data for the prediction model:

# Rebuild all features
team_win_rate <- matches %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(team1, team2),
               names_to = "role", values_to = "team") %>%
  group_by(team) %>%
  summarise(
    total_matches = n(),
    total_wins    = sum(winner == team),
    win_rate      = round(total_wins / total_matches, 3)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(win_rate))

cat("Team Win Rates:\n")
## Team Win Rates:
print(team_win_rate)
## # A tibble: 15 × 4
##    team                        total_matches total_wins win_rate
##    <chr>                               <int>      <int>    <dbl>
##  1 Gujarat Titans                         45         28    0.622
##  2 Chennai Super Kings                   237        138    0.582
##  3 Lucknow Super Giants                   43         24    0.558
##  4 Mumbai Indians                        261        144    0.552
##  5 Kolkata Knight Riders                 251        131    0.522
##  6 Rajasthan Royals                      219        112    0.511
##  7 Rising Pune Supergiants                30         15    0.5  
##  8 Royal Challengers Bengaluru           252        123    0.488
##  9 Sunrisers Hyderabad                   182         88    0.484
## 10 Delhi Capitals                        250        115    0.46 
## 11 Punjab Kings                          246        112    0.455
## 12 Gujarat Lions                          30         13    0.433
## 13 Kochi Tuskers Kerala                   14          6    0.429
## 14 Deccan Chargers                        75         29    0.387
## 15 Pune Warriors                          45         12    0.267
all_teams     <- sort(unique(c(matches$team1, matches$team2)))
team_encoding <- setNames(1:length(all_teams), all_teams)
venue_counts  <- matches %>%
  group_by(venue) %>%
  summarise(venue_match_count = n())

model_features <- read_csv("../data/model_features.csv") %>%
  mutate(team1_wins = factor(team1_wins,
                             levels = c("Team2_Wins", "Team1_Wins")))

cat("\nModel features shape:",
    nrow(model_features), "rows,",
    ncol(model_features), "cols\n")
## 
## Model features shape: 1090 rows, 12 cols
cat("\nTarget variable distribution:\n")
## 
## Target variable distribution:
print(round(prop.table(table(model_features$team1_wins)) * 100, 1))
## 
## Team2_Wins Team1_Wins 
##       49.1       50.9

7 Machine Learning Models

7.1 Train-Test Split

set.seed(42)
train_index <- createDataPartition(model_features$team1_wins,
                                   p = 0.80, list = FALSE)
train_data  <- model_features[ train_index, ]
test_data   <- model_features[-train_index, ]

cat("Training rows:", nrow(train_data), "\n")
## Training rows: 872
cat("Testing rows :", nrow(test_data),  "\n")
## Testing rows : 218

7.2 Cross Validation Setup

train_control <- trainControl(
  method          = "cv",
  number          = 10,
  savePredictions = TRUE,
  classProbs      = TRUE,
  summaryFunction = twoClassSummary
)

7.3 Model 1 — Logistic Regression

set.seed(42)
model_lr <- train(
  team1_wins ~ ., data = train_data,
  method = "glm", family = "binomial",
  trControl = train_control, metric = "ROC"
)
pred_lr <- predict(model_lr, newdata = test_data)
cm_lr   <- confusionMatrix(pred_lr, test_data$team1_wins,
                            positive = "Team1_Wins")
acc_lr  <- round(cm_lr$overall["Accuracy"] * 100, 2)
cat("Logistic Regression Accuracy:", acc_lr, "%\n")
## Logistic Regression Accuracy: 53.67 %

7.4 Model 2 — Decision Tree

set.seed(42)
model_dt <- train(
  team1_wins ~ ., data = train_data,
  method = "rpart", trControl = train_control,
  metric = "ROC", tuneLength = 10
)
pred_dt <- predict(model_dt, newdata = test_data)
cm_dt   <- confusionMatrix(pred_dt, test_data$team1_wins,
                            positive = "Team1_Wins")
acc_dt  <- round(cm_dt$overall["Accuracy"] * 100, 2)
cat("Decision Tree Accuracy:", acc_dt, "%\n")
## Decision Tree Accuracy: 55.5 %
rpart.plot(
  model_dt$finalModel,
  type          = 4,
  extra         = 108,
  box.palette   = "RdYlGn",
  shadow.col    = "gray80",
  fallen.leaves = TRUE,
  main          = "IPL Match Outcome — Decision Tree"
)

Decision Tree structure for IPL match prediction

7.5 Model 3 — Random Forest

set.seed(42)
model_rf <- train(
  team1_wins ~ ., data = train_data,
  method = "rf", trControl = train_control,
  metric = "ROC", tuneLength = 5, ntree = 500
)
pred_rf <- predict(model_rf, newdata = test_data)
cm_rf   <- confusionMatrix(pred_rf, test_data$team1_wins,
                            positive = "Team1_Wins")
acc_rf  <- round(cm_rf$overall["Accuracy"] * 100, 2)
cat("Random Forest Accuracy:", acc_rf, "%\n")
## Random Forest Accuracy: 51.83 %

8 Model Evaluation

8.1 Accuracy Comparison

model_comparison <- data.frame(
  Model    = c("Logistic Regression", "Decision Tree", "Random Forest"),
  Accuracy = c(acc_lr, acc_dt, acc_rf),
  Sensitivity = c(
    round(cm_lr$byClass["Sensitivity"] * 100, 2),
    round(cm_dt$byClass["Sensitivity"] * 100, 2),
    round(cm_rf$byClass["Sensitivity"] * 100, 2)
  ),
  Specificity = c(
    round(cm_lr$byClass["Specificity"] * 100, 2),
    round(cm_dt$byClass["Specificity"] * 100, 2),
    round(cm_rf$byClass["Specificity"] * 100, 2)
  )
)

print(model_comparison)
##                 Model Accuracy Sensitivity Specificity
## 1 Logistic Regression    53.67       56.76       50.47
## 2       Decision Tree    55.50       60.36       50.47
## 3       Random Forest    51.83       43.24       60.75
ggplot(model_comparison,
       aes(x = reorder(Model, Accuracy),
           y = Accuracy, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(Accuracy, "%")),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Logistic Regression" = "#0072B2",
    "Decision Tree"       = "#E69F00",
    "Random Forest"       = "#009E73"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Model Accuracy Comparison",
    x = "Model", y = "Accuracy (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  ylim(0, 100)

Accuracy comparison across all three models

8.2 Feature Importance

importance_df <- varImp(model_rf)$importance %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("feature") %>%
  arrange(desc(Overall))

ggplot(importance_df,
       aes(x = reorder(feature, Overall),
           y = Overall, fill = Overall)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#56B4E9", high = "#E69F00") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Overall, 1)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  labs(
    title = "Feature Importance — Random Forest",
    x = "Feature", y = "Importance Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  ylim(0, max(importance_df$Overall) * 1.15)

Most important features according to Random Forest

8.3 ROC Curve

library(pROC)
pred_rf_prob <- predict(model_rf, newdata = test_data, type = "prob")
roc_rf <- roc(
  response  = test_data$team1_wins,
  predictor = pred_rf_prob[, "Team1_Wins"],
  levels    = c("Team2_Wins", "Team1_Wins")
)
plot(roc_rf,
     col             = "#009E73",
     lwd             = 2.5,
     main            = paste("ROC Curve — Random Forest | AUC =",
                             round(auc(roc_rf), 3)),
     print.auc       = TRUE,
     auc.polygon     = TRUE,
     auc.polygon.col = "#009E7330")

ROC Curve for Random Forest model

9 Key Findings & Conclusions

9.1 Summary of Findings

Finding Insight
Most wins Mumbai Indians — 144 wins
Best win rate Gujarat Titans — 62.2%
Toss impact Only 50.8% — nearly random
Fielding first preference 64% of toss winners chose to field
Top batsman Virat Kohli — 8,014 runs
Top bowler YS Chahal — 205 wickets
Most used venue Eden Gardens — 77 matches
Best model Decision Tree — 55.50% accuracy
Top feature Win rate difference — Score 100

9.2 Model Performance Discussion

The best model achieved 55.50% accuracy using only pre-match features. While this may appear modest, it represents a meaningful improvement over the 50% baseline (random guessing) for the following reasons:

  • Cricket is inherently unpredictable — upsets are frequent
  • Only 11 pre-match features were used — no live ball-by-ball data
  • Professional cricket analytics systems use real-time data and achieve 80–85% accuracy using in-match features like current score and wickets fallen

9.3 Limitations

  1. No live match data — only pre-match features used
  2. Venue name mismatches — some newer venues not found in dataset (e.g. Narendra Modi Stadium)
  3. Retired teams — Pune Warriors and Kochi Tuskers Kerala have very few matches, affecting their win rate reliability
  4. Team composition changes — same team name across years can have very different squads

9.4 Future Improvements

  1. Add ball-by-ball live features like powerplay score and wickets to significantly improve accuracy
  2. Use player availability and recent form as features
  3. Apply deep learning models like LSTM for sequence-based match prediction
  4. Build a Shiny web app for interactive match prediction

10 Conclusion

This project successfully performed end-to-end IPL data analysis covering data loading, cleaning, exploratory analysis, feature engineering, and machine learning model building. Three models were trained and compared — Decision Tree emerged as the best performer with 55.50% accuracy.

The analysis revealed that team historical strength (win rate difference) and venue familiarity are the strongest predictors of match outcomes, while toss results have minimal impact. Virat Kohli and Yuzvendra Chahal stand out as the greatest individual performers in IPL history.

The live match prediction function successfully predicts outcomes for any combination of IPL teams, providing probability scores alongside the predicted winner.

Report generated using R 4.5.3 | Subject: R for Data Science
